unitTitle: KS2 Y4 Data logging (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Answering questions — data over time
lessons.objectives:
I can say what data is
I can choose the right table to answer a question
I can think of a question a table could answer
I can spot data that is collected over time
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: data · table · heading · over time. TA: everyone has the starter + activity worksheet. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — data around us. Pupils look at the school register example and CHOOSE that it is data collected over time, and what it can tell us.
   • Support: what is data?  • Core: one thing the register shows.  • Challenge: why collect it over time.
2. (5–15) I-do → we-do — three tables (A weather, B fitness tracker, C sport). Headings tell you what each holds. Pupils CHOOSE which table answers each question (A/B/C, or X if none can). Likely error: pick any table → fix-words "check the headings first; if it's not a heading the answer is X."
3. (15–20) Classroom data — what could we ask if we recorded temperature, sound and light in the room? Build a class list (kept for Lesson 5).
4. (20–35) You-do — count the cars. Teacher plays a 1-minute road video (motion only). Each pupil counts ONE car colour and tallies. This is collecting data over time. Card-sort: collected over time vs one-off.
   • Support: do the first sort together.  • Core: which question has no answer (X).  • Challenge: write a new question Table B could answer.
5. (35–40) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil names a question their tally could answer.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody's screen looks less done. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine. NOTE: the source A/B/C tables are PowerPoint tables (not rasters) — recreated as small markdown tables in the worksheet; a clean combined image is logged as an image-gap.

resources.title: Cars on a road (Teach Computing).png

lessons.title: Data collection — sensors and data loggers
lessons.objectives:
I can say what a sensor is
I can name the sensors on a data logger
I can read the data logger display
I can say that sensors can record data automatically
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: input device · sensor · data logger. TA: everyone has the worksheets; a data logger to handle if available. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — senses to sensors. People use ears/eyes; a computer uses SENSORS. A microphone can be a SOUND sensor. Pupils CHOOSE what a microphone senses, and match each sensor to its job. Stress: a sensor is an INPUT.
   • Support: what are ears for.  • Core: match sensor to job.  • Challenge: explain what a sound sensor does.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — the data logger. It has sound, light and temperature sensors. Pupils LABEL the data logger (drag labels onto the icons + screen + buttons). TA: point at each icon.
3. (25–35) Read the display — sound in dBA, light in lx, temperature in °C. Pupils read values off the screen (choose + fill-blank). Likely error: muddling units → fix-words "dBA = sound, lx = light, °C = temperature."
4. (35–50) You-do — recording automatically. Connect a logger; the computer records readings and makes a graph. Card-sort the sensors to their jobs. Challenge: why is leaving a logger on its own useful?
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil points to one sensor.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Logging — data points and intervals
lessons.objectives:
I can say what a data point is
I can say what an interval is
I can read a value from a data table
I can read a value from a line graph
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: logging · data point · interval. TA: everyone has the worksheets; set a logger recording at the start so there is data to download later. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — be the data logger. Pupils watch a day clip and write the temperature every hour. Each number is a DATA POINT; the gap between them is the INTERVAL.
   • Support: logging = writing numbers over time.  • Core: what was the interval.  • Challenge: why a logger (50/sec) beats a person.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — reading a data table. Each row is a data point; the time counts up in small steps (0.1 s). Pupils read a value (fill-blank: sound at 00.000 = 41.2 dBA). Likely error: muddling data point and interval → fix-words "a data point is a NUMBER; the interval is the GAP."
3. (25–40) Reading a line graph — the data points join into a line. Pupils find the lowest dip and describe the change. ORDER block: the steps of logging then downloading.
   • Support: one row = one data point.  • Core: when was light lowest.  • Challenge: describe the change between two times.
4. (40–50) You-do — download the data the logger has collected since the start; find a data point.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil shows one data point on the graph.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Analysing data — graphs, tables and sorting
lessons.objectives:
I can open and view saved data
I can read a value from a graph and a table
I can sort data to find the highest and lowest
I can say there are different ways to view data
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: analyse · sort · highest · lowest. TA: the cooling data file open in the logger software + a spreadsheet ready. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — predict. Hot water is left out and a sensor records its temperature for 5 hours. Pupils predict the temperature falls and the line goes down.
   • Support: hot water gets colder.  • Core: why save data to share.  • Challenge: why a computer makes analysing easier.
2. (5–25) I-do → we-do — the cooling graph. Read the highest value (≈50°C), see the line fall, fill-blank the value at 60 minutes (≈38°C). Support: trace the line with a finger.
3. (25–35) The same data as a table — graph vs table show the SAME data. Pupils choose which shows change at a glance (graph) and say which they prefer.
4. (35–50) You-do — sort in a spreadsheet. SORT puts numbers in order; smallest-to-largest puts the lowest at the top. Pupils find the highest and lowest. Likely error: thinking sort changes the data → fix-words "sorting just re-orders it; the numbers stay the same." TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil names the highest and lowest temperature.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Data for answers — plan a data logging experiment
lessons.objectives:
I can write a question a data logger could answer
I can choose the right sensor
I can plan where to put the logger and how long to log for
I can set up a data logger to collect data
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: question · sensor · plan · collect. TA: data loggers ready to set going; the worksheets open. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — which sensor? Match each question to the right sensor (brightest → light, loudest → sound, coldest → temperature). Likely error: choosing a sensor that can't answer the question → fix-words "match the sensor to the question word."
   • Support: warm = temperature sensor.  • Core: write a sound-sensor question.  • Challenge: why choose the sensor first.
2. (10–25) I-do → we-do — asking a question. A good question can be answered with data. Pupils write their own question (use the class list from Lesson 1). Choose the sensor.
3. (25–40) You-do — make a plan. ORDER the set-up steps, then fill the plan: where the logger goes, when it starts/stops, how long, and a prediction. Agree a common start/stop time to manage devices.
4. (40–50) Test it — set the logger going for a minute and check it works. Loggers then collect data until next lesson.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four "I can…"; each pupil states their question and sensor.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Data logger (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Answering my question — drawing conclusions
lessons.objectives:
I can find and review my collected data
I can use my data to answer my question
I can write a conclusion from my data
I can say why a data logger is useful
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: review · analyse · conclusion · benefit. TA: each pupil's data file open (or downloaded ready); the report worksheet open. Read the four "I can…" aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — read a graph to draw a conclusion. A light graph dips twice; pupils read that the room got darker at those times and that it mostly stayed ≈280 lx.
   • Support: line down = darker.  • Core: write a conclusion the graph shows.  • Challenge: what could have caused a dip.
2. (10–25) Review your data — open the data your logger collected; check it is YOUR data; find the highest and lowest. TA: remind them how to hide/zoom.
3. (25–45) You-do — write your report. Question, prediction, highest/lowest, the answer to your question, and whether it matched your prediction. Calm independent work; prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (45–52) Why use a data logger? Multi-select the benefits (collects on its own; works when you're not there; same way each time) — NOT "does your writing for you." Think-pair-share.
5. (52–55) End of unit — recap the journey: question → choose sensor → log over time → analyse → conclusion. Self-tick the four "I can…".
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
